questions.question: How is anxiety best described at an awareness level?
options: A sign that a person is being difficult or attention-seeking
A rare condition that only affects people with a physical illness
A feeling of unease, worry or fear that everyone experiences from time to time
A deliberate choice a person makes to avoid activities

questions.question: The body's natural readiness for action when it senses a threat is often called the:
options: "stop and think" response
"fight or flight" response
"wait and see" response
"rest and digest" response

questions.question: In small amounts, ordinary anxiety can be:
options: Useful, keeping a person alert and helping them prepare
Something that never passes once it starts
A reason to keep someone away from all stressful events
Harmful and always a sign of illness

questions.question: Worry is more likely to be an anxiety disorder when the feelings:
options: Ease as soon as a stressful event is over
Last only a few minutes before an appointment
Only ever appear in young adults
Are strong, last for weeks or months and interfere with everyday life

questions.question: Which statement about how common anxiety is among the people you support is accurate?
options: Anxiety can affect anyone and can begin at any age
Older people cannot develop anxiety
Anxiety only affects people who already have a formal diagnosis
Anxiety only affects people with no physical health problems

questions.question: Why is anxiety often missed in the people you support?
options: Care workers are never in a position to notice changes
It only ever shows as a single panic attack
It always produces obvious, dramatic behaviour
People may feel ashamed and hide it, and signs can be mistaken for physical illness

questions.question: Which three words best sum up your supportive role in anxiety?
options: Recognise, respond and signpost
Counsel, analyse and cure
Assess, prescribe and review
Diagnose, treat and discharge

questions.question: Which action stays within the proper limits of a care worker's role?
options: Telling the person which anxiety condition they have
Deciding what medication the person should take
Providing therapy to explain away the person's fears
Sharing concerns and encouraging professional help

questions.question: Which of the following best describes generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?
options: Worry that lasts only a few minutes before a stressful event
An intense fear of one specific object such as spiders
A single panic attack that happens once and never returns
Ongoing, hard-to-control worry that spreads across many areas of life over a long period

questions.question: Which physical signs are commonly linked with generalised anxiety disorder because the body stays on alert?
options: A complete loss of any worried thoughts
Improved sleep and boundless energy
A total absence of physical symptoms
Headaches, tiredness and a churning stomach

questions.question: A panic attack is best described as:
options: A permanent physical illness of the heart
A sudden rush of intense fear and physical sensations that peaks within minutes
A gentle feeling of calm that comes and goes
A slow build-up of mild worry lasting several weeks

questions.question: Which statement about the physical sensations of a panic attack is correct?
options: They can only be stopped with medication
They are dangerous and cause lasting physical harm
They feel alarming but are not dangerous and will pass
They mean the person is definitely having a heart attack

questions.question: Panic disorder is best described as:
options: A one-off panic attack that never happens again
Ongoing worry about many different areas of life
A fear of a single specific object such as needles
Repeated panic attacks together with worry about having more

questions.question: How can repeated panic attacks begin to narrow a person's life?
options: By curing their anxiety completely on its own
By removing all fear so they take more risks
By leading them to dread the next attack and avoid places where one happened before
By making the person seek out crowded places more often

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a phobia?
options: Reliving a past traumatic event through flashbacks
Wide-ranging worry about many areas of life at once
An intense, overwhelming fear of a particular object or situation that is out of proportion to real danger
A repeated ritual carried out to reduce distress

questions.question: A person refuses to join the day centre, saying they are terrified of being judged and humiliated in front of the group. This is most consistent with:
options: Deliberate refusal that is best ignored
A specific phobia of an object such as heights
Social anxiety — an overwhelming fear of social situations
Ordinary shyness that needs no support

questions.question: In obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), the unwanted, distressing thoughts, images or urges are known as:
options: Flashbacks
Obsessions
Compulsions
Phobias

questions.question: In OCD, the repeated actions or rituals a person feels driven to carry out to ease their anxiety are known as:
options: Obsessions
Grounding techniques
Compulsions
Triggers

questions.question: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) can develop:
options: Purely because a person chooses to dwell on the past
After a frightening or distressing event such as an accident, assault or serious illness
Only in people who have never experienced difficulty
Only in children and never in older adults

questions.question: What is the most appropriate approach when supporting someone who has PTSD?
options: Encourage them to describe their traumatic memories in full detail
Ignore any signs of distress so as not to make a fuss
Approach with patience, keep to reassuring routines and report signs of distress
Insist they confront their fears straight away

questions.question: What does person-centred support for anxiety mean in practice?
options: Learning what anxiety feels like for this person and shaping care around their wishes
Deciding for the person what will make them feel better
Applying the same fixed routine to everyone
Avoiding the care plan so support stays flexible

questions.question: How should you use your own manner when someone is anxious?
options: Show your frustration so they understand the urgency
Raise your voice so they can focus on you
Stay calm, speak slowly and gently, and keep your body language open and relaxed
Speak quickly and firmly to snap them out of it

questions.question: A person you support is having a panic attack, with a racing heart and feeling unable to breathe. What is the most helpful response?
options: Stay calm, reassure them they are safe and it will pass, and gently encourage slow breathing
Tell them firmly to pull themselves together
Leave them completely alone at once and say nothing
Insist they describe in detail everything that is frightening them

questions.question: Which best describes a grounding technique you might gently offer someone whose anxiety feels overwhelming?
options: Telling them to think harder about what frightens them
Asking them to hold their breath for as long as possible
Inviting them to name things they can see, hear and touch around them
Leaving them to work through it entirely on their own

questions.question: Which statement about treatment for anxiety is accurate at awareness level?
options: Anxiety cannot be treated, so there is little point encouraging help
People should be pressured into treatment whether they want it or not
Care workers should decide which therapy or medication a person needs
Anxiety disorders are treatable, and encouraging someone to speak to their GP is a helpful step

questions.question: Anxiety is a feeling of unease, worry or fear that everyone experiences from time to time.
options: True
False

questions.question: In small amounts, anxiety can be useful, keeping a person alert and helping them prepare.
options: True
False

questions.question: A panic attack, though frightening, is not physically dangerous and the feelings do pass.
options: True
False

questions.question: A phobia is simply a mild preference to avoid something and rarely affects everyday life.
options: True
False

questions.question: Social anxiety disorder is exactly the same as ordinary shyness and needs no support.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is a care worker's job to decide which medication a person with anxiety should take.
options: True
False

questions.question: Looking after your own mental wellbeing helps keep you able to care well for others.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should always tell an anxious person to simply "stop worrying" so they can move on.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are common physical symptoms of a panic attack? (Select all that apply)
options: Shortness of breath
A racing heart
A permanently slowed pulse
A complete absence of any physical sensation
Dizziness, sweating or shaking

questions.question: Which of the following are part of the Five Ways to Wellbeing? (Select all that apply)
options: Avoid all contact with other people
Be active
Keep learning
Connect with others
Take notice

questions.question: Which changes might suggest someone you support is struggling with anxiety they cannot easily put into words? (Select all that apply)
options: Seeming restless
Seeking frequent reassurance
Becoming withdrawn
Consistently feeling calmer and more sociable than usual
Being reluctant to leave their room

questions.question: How can you help someone through a panic attack? (Select all that apply)
options: Insist they explain every frightening thought in detail
Stay with them and reassure them they are safe and it will pass
Tell them firmly to hurry up and get on with the day
Gently encourage slow breathing, breathing with them
Reduce noise, onlookers and pressure

questions.question: Which of the following are things a care worker should NOT do when supporting someone with anxiety? (Select all that apply)
options: Encourage the person gently towards professional help
Pressure the person into a feared situation
Tell the person which anxiety condition they have
Notice and record changes in mood or behaviour
Make decisions about therapy or medication

questions.question: Which of the following are good practice when recording and escalating concerns about anxiety? (Select all that apply)
options: Keep concerns to yourself so as not to worry anyone
Write down your own guesses about a diagnosis instead of the facts
Raise any mention of self-harm or feeling unable to go on straight away
Report concerns promptly to your senior, the nurse or the care plan
Record the facts — what you saw or heard, when, and what helped

questions.question: Which of the following are ways you can support a person's everyday wellbeing, at their own pace? (Select all that apply)
options: Discourage all contact with other people
Support contact with family, friends and others
Encourage them to stay in bed as much as possible each day
Encourage gentle movement and fresh air
Help with good sleep, regular meals and a predictable routine

questions.question: A sudden rush of intense fear with physical symptoms that peaks within minutes is a ___ attack.
The body's natural readiness for action when it senses a threat is sometimes called the "___ or flight" response.
Ongoing, hard-to-control worry that spreads across many areas of life over a long period is called ___ anxiety disorder.
A talking therapy that helps people understand and manage anxious thoughts, cognitive behavioural therapy, is often shortened to ___.
The usual first step in helping someone access professional support for anxiety is to encourage a conversation with their ___.
